data.title: GMS Is The Right Fit For QXO's Long-Term Goal
data.description:
The QXO and GMS Investment Thesis

QXO, Inc. (NYSE:QXO) is a promising company that aims to acquire a large share of the construction industry market through aggressive M&A. And the latest acquisition rumors regarding GMS Inc. (NYSE:GMS) have brought QXO back into the spotlight.

I believe this planned acquisition will increase value, and I am convinced by QXO's strategy. Therefore, despite the fact that they might appear expensive to traditional value investors, I consider the stock to be promising.

The strategy of QXO and how I became aware of it

Last October, I wrote an article about Beacon Roofing Supply (BECN) because I found their capital allocation strategy very interesting. Shortly thereafter, they were acquired by QXC, which I covered in an article from March 2025, shortly before the deal was finalized. This led me to take a closer look at QXO.

Because I place great importance on capital allocation and management expertise, I was particularly impressed by Brad Jacobs's résumé. He has achieved significant success with XPO (XPO), United Rentals (URI), and United Waste Systems (WM), as well as their spinoffs, and has generated a lot of shareholder value.

In my opinion, the most important factors for the success were the exceptional M&A activities and the development of highly talented employees who perform exceptionally well as part of a team. In other words, I clearly see QXO as a bet on Brad Jacobs and the culture of his company providing a competitive advantage.

To build the right team, Ashwin Rao was brought in, who previously worked as Vice President of AI at Target (TGT). He has the necessary expertise to establish AI-driven demand forecasting and pricing as a competitive advantage. Furthermore, Ihsan Essaid was hired as CFO who previously was the Global Head of M&A at Barclays. So another person with high expertise in his field.

QXO's plan is now to consolidate the construction industry and optimize business processes with the help of AI. The first step was the acquisition of Beacon Roofing Supply, which now gives QXO access to more than 500 locations in the US and Canada. The goal, however, is to reach an annual revenue of $50 billion within a decade. So to achieve this goal, further acquisitions are necessary, as Beacon Roofing Supply only generated about $10 billion annually.

What impact did the Beacon Roofing Supply deal have on liquidity?

To finance the acquisition, QXO completed a $2.25 billion offering of 6.75% Senior Secured Notes due 2032. Jacobs acknowledged the sizable debt but was quick to note that deleveraging is a priority. “We’re targeting a 4x net leverage ratio by 2026. That’s very achievable given Beacon’s cash flow profile and our operating improvements,” he said.

Assuming the net debt of 4x is reached by 2026, I would consider the liquidity/debt position to be strong. This is especially true given my belief that the roofing materials industry has a bright future.

What are the plans for the future, and what is GMS's role in this context?

QXO has made an offer to purchase all of GMS's outstanding shares at $95.20 each, which would equate to approximately $5 billion. This tender offer is available until June 24. After that, the offer will be made directly to the GMS shareholders.

With over 300 locations and approximately 100 tool sales, rental, and service centers currently owned by GMS, QXO could establish a strong presence in the construction products industry, potentially leading to future dominance. GMS has also frequently expanded its product range and geographical reach through roll-ups, which I believe strengthens its alignment with QXO.

However, since Brad Jacobs's playbook is known and the established players on the market would naturally like to maintain their competitive position, there are now additional reports that Home Depot (HD) could also be interested in GMS. I think this is probably an attempt to prevent QXO from growing too quickly and becoming dominant in the market. Of course, GMS would also fit with Home Depot. However, I believe it is more likely the latter. Plus it could also be an attempt to drive up the price that QXO must pay.

Now that other major players are involved, the deals may not be as lucrative as before. But the same was thought in the other industries Brad Jacobs has successfully entered. But as is often the case, a first-class management team is the most important competitive advantage.

Valuation

Since investing always involves looking into the future, and since QXO's goal is to reach revenues of $50 billion, we can use peer comparisons to determine the price at which QXO would trade. Since competitors usually have a PS multiple of 1–2x, this would likely make QXO worth between $50 and $100 billion. This would be a huge increase from the current $13 billion.

However, this also means that QXO is currently trading at a premium compared to Builder FirstSource (BLDR), with a PS ratio likely in the range of ~1.3x. But this also demonstrates the market's confidence in Brad Jacobs's abilities. Because if the market now believes that a multiple in the range of Lowe's is appropriate, this is a sign of appreciation.

Conclusion

Should the QXO/GMS deal come to fruition, I believe it would be highly valuable for QXO, as it aligns with the strategy outlined by the management team. Additionally, more acquisitions will probably follow in the coming years, all of which aim to consolidate the market.

I have a lot of respect for Brad Jacobs' abilities, so I will build a small entry position over the next few days to continue observing the situation. But I am also aware that the shares are not currently cheap and that this is a bet on the management team and its capital allocation.

data.title: Developed International Small-Cap Stocks (Unlike Their U.S. Counterparts) Are Outperforming This Year
data.description:
By James Picerno

The investment case for small-cap stocks has caught a lot of grief in recent years, and for an obvious reason: performance has been lackluster, particularly when measured against large-cap shares. No one anticipates consistent outperformance, but the motivating thesis for owning small caps is that this slice of the equities market is expected to outperform over the medium to long term. That was mostly true in the years following the landmark 1981 paper that found a “size effect” in stocks from 1936 through 1975. But the small-cap premium has faded.

For example, the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) has trailed its large-cap counterpart (SPY) by a hefty margin over the trailing 5- and 10-year periods, according to Morningstar.com.

The chart below reminds that small caps had been posting strong performance over large caps. But the leadership topped out during the 2012-2019 period, and since then, small caps have fallen behind large caps with a fair amount regularity, according to a ratio for a pair of ETFs that proxy for the two markets.

The weaker performance in small caps can be explained by several factors, but perhaps the most compelling is the rise of big tech in recent history. As the largest tech firms – think Apple, Microsoft, Google, etc. – have increased their dominance, their market leadership positions have become increasingly profitable. Investors, in turn, have rewarded the so-called Magnificent 7 stocks with higher valuations and prices.

Add in cost-intensive efforts related to artificial intelligence (AI) into the mix and big tech’s advantages become even more striking. In turn, smaller firms are having a tougher time competing in the tech-fueled new world order.

Is the small-cap effect dead? Unclear, but it has certainly turned dormant lately. Generalizing about small-caps, however, can overlook opportunity. For example, the small-cap premia look more durable in foreign markets. Small-cap ex-US performance in developed markets (ISCF) has a stronger record of posting outperformance vs. an international large and mid-cap benchmark (EFA), compared with the US equivalent, as the next chart using ETF proxies suggests. Part of the reason may be that the Magnificent 7 effect in the US has no counterpart (or at least nothing directly comparable) in foreign developed markets.

Year to date, developed-world small-cap is showing renewed leadership over its large-cap counterparts. ICSF in 2025 is up 18.5%, a moderate premium relative to EFA’s 17.2% increase, as of June 18.

The international small-cap premium may have further room to run. For starters, international equities generally have only recently started to outperform US stocks after a long period of American leadership. If this marks a secular shift, foreign equities could be poised for several years of superior results – a scenario that, presumably, would fuel the ongoing strength in international small caps over their large-cap brethren.

Second, small-cap valuations are relatively low, which implies higher expected returns. ICSF’s trailing price-earnings ratio is roughly 12.0, according to Morninstar.com. That’s moderately below the 14.8 p/e for developed market stocks (EFA) and the 14.5 p/e for US small caps (IJR). The discount is far more dramatic compared with the lofty 22.3 p/e for US big-caps (SPY).

Recent price momentum and valuation, in other words, favor international small caps. That’s no guarantee, but the comparisons suggest that a tailwind could continue to blow in favor of the small-cap effect ex-US.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GeneDx Holdings: A More Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
Shares of genomics testing concern GeneDx Holdings Corp. (NASDAQ:WGS) are down nearly by half since it posted top and bottom-line beats in its Q1 '25 financial report and raised FY25 guidance on April 30th. After a meteoric run in less than 18 months, the market was disappointed in the sequential decline in exome/genomic tests Q1 '25 vs. Q4 '24, although seasonality played a role. The company did just launch a new subscription service around treatments for erectile dysfunction or ED.

With excellent secular tailwinds and a strong foothold in genomics testing buttressed by an extensive database, the still rich valuations and recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

GeneDx Holdings Corp. is a Stamford, Connecticut based genomics testing and data concern, having sequenced over 750,000 exomes/genomes since inception. It boasts both the ability to screen for ~10,000 rare diseases and a database comprised of more than six million structured phenotypes. GeneDx was formed in 2000 by two National Institute of Health scientists and acquired by Bio-Reference Laboratories in 2006, later becoming part of OPKO Health (OPK) in 2015. OPKO sold GeneDx to struggling Sema4 for a cash consideration of $150 million and 80 million shares of stock in 2022 with its first trade post-transaction conducted at $2,319.57 a share, after giving effect to a 1-for-33 reverse stock split in 2023.

With Sema4 shutting down its reproductive services business to focus on genomic testing in late 2022, it assumed the moniker of its acquiree in 2023. Shares of WGS trade just under $65.00 a share, equating to an approximate market cap of $1.85 billion.

Sequencing

Regarding its testing, the company’s primary focus is epilepsy, autism, intellectual disabilities, congenital anomalies, and rare diseases. Although it exome/genome sequencing accounted for approximately one-third of its 226,232 processed panels in FY24, it was responsible for over three-quarters of GeneDx’s top line. For those unaware, genome sequencing encompasses all ~3 billion base pairs in humans, whereas exome panels involve analyzing only the 20,000 to 25,000 protein coding genes, comprising 1% to 2% of the entire genome.

Despite all the ballyhoo regarding biomarker assays, genetic sequencing and testing, a rare disease patient will be misdiagnosed an average of three times before receiving an accurate genetic reading, prolonging a proper diagnosis by up to six years. With surprisingly low diagnostic rates of under 50%, exome/genome sequencing is still the most accurate test for disease detection. This is not simply the fault of the tests but is also a function of disease complexity. For example, at least 768 genes are related to seizures, but less than half of those are tested for on most commercial epilepsy panels, whereas the far from full-proof exome/genome sequencing at least assays all 768 genes.

To improve accuracy and quicken diagnosis, GeneDx is leveraging AI and its voluminous database to separate itself from the competition through multiple avenues, including affordable (i.e., covered by insurance) tests with quick turnaround times (days or weeks) and increasingly accurate interpretations to drive therapeutic next steps. Each new test produces additional data, leading to progressively more informed assessments. One demographic the company is pursuing with is its ever-expanding data network is newborns. They are traditionally tested for 37 conditions with biomarkers, whereas GeneDx’s sequencing can screen for ~450. Loosely translated, 92% of disorders detected by sequencing would not be found by biomarker analysis, meaning they could go undetected and advance unchecked for years.

Able to sell the superiority of its ever-improving approach with quicker and more accurate diagnoses, GeneDx signed on 32 state Medicaid programs to cover exome and genome sequencing in the pediatric outpatient setting and 14 for rapid genome sequencing in the neonatal intensive care unit through 2024. Currently, less than 5% of infants get a genetic test, but owing to this escalating coverage, the company has screened 17,000 newborns, making it the market leader. That said, denial rates from insurance coverage are currently in the mid-40 percent area, leaving plenty of room for improvement.

Believing that its proprietary data set enriched for rare and pediatric diseases provides a significant barrier to entry, management places the market opportunity for these indications at ~$25 billion, including its use in the clinic to reduce drug development costs by cutting timelines and amplifying rates of success – noteworthy, considering that only ~5% of rare diseases have an approved therapy available to patients.

Q1 '25 Financials, Revised FY25 Outlook, & Share Price Performance

With a strong foothold in a rapidly growing market with multiple exome expansion opportunities (e.g., immune deficiency disorders, hearing loss), 56% revenue growth in FY24, and gross margins between 60% and 70%, shares of WGS rocketed from an all-time low of $1.16 in November 2023 to an all-time high (post the onboarding of GeneDx by Sema4) of $117.50 on April 28, 2025, representing a spellbinding 10,029% rally in under 18 months. Put another way, it went from trading at a price-to-FY23 sales of 0.15 to trading at 113 times FY25E EPS when it reported 1Q25 financials on April 30, 2025.

On that date, GeneDx posted earnings of $0.27 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $87.1 million versus a loss of $0.31 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $62.4 million in 1Q24, representing gains of 187% and 40% while beating Street estimates by $0.05 and $7.3 million, respectively. More importantly, revenue from exome/genome sequencing was up 62% to $71.4 million while total GAAP gross margin was 67.1% versus 59.9% in the prior year period. Its average reimbursement rate for exome/genome tests after all denials was $3,472 versus $2,651 in 1Q24. Furthermore, New Mexico became the 33rd Medicaid state to cover exome and genome sequencing in the pediatric outpatient setting.

Based on range midpoints, on the back of this solid performance, management raised its FY25 Adj. gross margin forecast from 66% to 67% and revenue outlook from $355 million to $367.5 million, of which $4 million is due to its pending $33 million acquisition of AI-powered genomic interpretation concern Fabric Genomics.

In response to what appeared to be a very solid quarter, shares of WGS cratered 43% to $66.85 in the subsequent trading session.

Besides for GeneDx’s somewhat priced-for-perfection valuation and a revenue beat (versus Street expectations) that was less than 10% for the first time in five quarters, the market’s severe reaction was a function of exome/genome panel volumes being down sequentially (0.55%) for the first time since the company went public. That said, 1Q25 had two fewer days (one less sales day) than 4Q24 and GeneDx typically experiences higher volume in 4Q and lower volume in 1Q due to seasonal demand from patients who have met their annual insurance deductible. There was one other concern: the reimbursement rate after all denials fell notably on a sequential basis from $3,811 to $3,472, but that was attributable to a multi-year appeal win from one of its largest national payers in 4Q24 and not a function of a change in rates.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Also, there is nothing wrong with the company’s balance sheet, which reflected cash and securities of $159.2 million against debt of $51.8 million on March 31, 2025. As a high-growth concern, GeneDx does not pay a dividend nor repurchase shares.

The analyst community is largely positive on the company. Since first quarter results were posted, five analyst firms including Jefferies, Guggenheim and BTIG have reissued Buy ratings. Price targets proffered range from $80 to $114 a share. Wells Fargo ($78 price target, down from $105 previously) maintained its Hold rating.

On average, they expect GeneDx to earn $1.06 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $368.6 million in FY25, followed by $1.91 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $437.8 million in FY26, representing sales gains of 80% and 18%, respectively.

Board member Keith Meister, representing the interests of beneficial owner Corvex Management, used the downdraft in GeneDx’s stock as an opportunity to add, purchasing 100,000 shares at an average price of $56.01 on May 8, 2025.

Verdict:

That said, the market’s revaluation of GeneDx now has it trading at 33.7 times FY26E EPS and 5.2 times FY25E sales. These are not cheap valuations but are somewhat warranted compared to the overall market considering the company’s considerable momentum, secular tailwinds propelling its offerings, and solid gross-margin profile.

Although GeneDx Holdings Corp. shares seem fairly valued despite the brutal adjustment, the sudden plummet in price has created solid premium in its stock options. It makes the stock worthy of a small "watch item" position using covered call orders to provide significant downside risk mitigation.

data.title: Cyrela: Paying You To Wait -- Deep Value In Brazil's High-End Homebuilde
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Lucas Martins as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis:

Cyrela Brazil Realty (OTCPK:CYRBY) is a high-margin, low-leverage home builder trading at barely 5 × earnings despite record launches in 2024, >20% ROE, and a 5% dividend yield. Its high-end mix and diversified brands cushion short-term rate shocks, while any turn in Brazil’s monetary cycle or continued earnings growth should rerate the stock toward 6–7 ×P/E, unlocking 25 %+ upside—all while investors are “paid to wait.”

Company Summary:

Cyrela Brazil Realty S.A. Empreendimentos e Participações (NYSE: CYRBY, BVMF: CYRE3) is one of the largest real estate developers and builders in Brazil with a market cap of US$1.66 billion, was founded in 1962 and has most of its revenue coming from high-end properties in São Paulo, the largest city in Latin America. Currently, it holds stakes in 9 companies related to the real estate sector, including them:

The company shows a high level of diversification, from low-income housing to real estate credit, and the luxury sector in Brazil, which represents Cyrela’s largest share, has been proving extremely resilient. The company started to recover after the crisis it faced in 2016 and went on to expand its launches strongly from 2019, achieving record highs in 2024. In that year, it launched a total of 54 developments, totaling PSV (gross sales value) of R$ 9.6 billion (US$ ~1.6 billion with a projected exchange rate of R$ 5.8), representing a 45% increase over 2023.

Economy & Sector:

The sector is in relative difficulty. This is mainly due to the macroeconomic situation that Brazil has been facing recently. The SELIC is at a target of 15, ranking as the third-highest real interest rate in the world. These high rates make construction costs more expensive overall and tend to reduce demand for real estate.

These interest rates are mainly caused by a fiscal risk that Brazil is facing, which leads to an accelerated inflation of 5.32% per year and this would directly affect Cyrela given the increase in production costs.

However, as the company has already demonstrated, despite these negative macro indicators, there are others that remain positive. The unemployment rate is at a very low level by Brazilian standards, only 6.6%, in line with the historical minimum. Meanwhile, the average income in Brazil stands at R$ 3,426, in line with the historical maximum.

Brazil's Unemployment Rate:

Brazil's Average income in R$:

Moreover, Brazil has a housing deficit of 5.9 million households; on average, each household houses 2.79 people in Brazil, which translates to almost 16.5 million Brazilians who effectively need to “move” due to housing conditions. This number, although declining, is still substantial and mostly concentrated in the states where Cyrela operates the most (São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro), and although this statistic does not directly affect Cyrela’s main segment (high-end), the company has smaller brands that operate in the low-income sector, aimed at meeting this demand.

So, in my view, on the one hand, the economy is showing signs that call for caution, and on the other hand, it shows positive signs for the company’s operations, which explains the record results presented in Q4 2024 despite the macro adversities. It is also worth noting that the high-end segment is not as affected by high interest rates, given that only 40% of its high/mid-income clients choose to use mortgage credit.

In addition to just Cyrela, the real estate market in Brazil as a whole is in a positive moment, as in the 1st quarter of 2025 residential property sales in the country grew by around 15.7%, showing that macro expectations may not be so determinative for the results.

Although the increase in the SELIC rate to 15% signals a contractionary period for an extended time, it is possible that rates at this level will not significantly impact the company in the long term, but may cause short-term adversities. However, once there are signs of the end of the contractionary period, this particular stock is expected to benefit significantly. (Especially if the Central Bank gives signs of rate cuts)

Financials:

Debt:

The company’s financials are solid. It currently has R$ 5.3 billion in cash and R$ 5.9 billion in debt, with shareholders’ equity of R$ 9.9 billion, indicating that net debt is only 7% of equity. With a net debt/EBITDA of just 0.33x, and most of the company’s debt linked not to the SELIC but to lower rates such as CRIs or those tied to the savings rate (TR), in my view, regardless of the macro outlook, the company will not face short-term problems with its debt.

Revenue:

The company’s revenue is well diversified across all income segments, demonstrating the strength of its diversification, even though the company chooses to focus on the high-end segment in order to capture higher margins and lower risks that this sector offers.

This effective diversification is reflected in year-over-year revenue growth, showing significant growth from 2021 to 2024. The growth is proportional to the launches and the speed at which the company sells them. The company managed to raise its sales speed to around 52% in 1Q25 and is expected to maintain around this level over the next 5 years (51%, 50%, 51%, 52%, and 53% respectively). This shows a high sales capacity compared to peers, where a speed of 30% is considered normal. It is the result of marketing optimizations, sales efforts, and proprietary apps that the company has developed in recent years.

The outlook for launches also remains positive. Co-CEO Raphael Horn said that 2025 should see growth in launches: “I think we are going to launch our entire schedule for this year, which means growth compared to last year,” meaning they expect to break the launch record once again. Since launches are generally proportional to revenue, in my opinion, this strongly indicates an increase in revenue as well.

Given the operational changes the company has implemented, along with its guidance, I have developed the following assumptions to project revenue and margins for 2025.

A slight deterioration compared to 2024 margins, but still satisfactory.

Valuation:

In my opinion, the company is cheap, even by Brazilian standards. It has a beta of just 0.47 and, despite excluding dividends, since 2022 it has performed significantly better than both the S&P500 and the IBOVESPA (the Brazilian stock exchange index), yet it still trades at very low multiples.

Multiples:

  • ROE: 21% (LTM)
  • P/B: 1.0x
  • P/E: 5.5x
  • P/A: 0.4x
  • P/S: 1.2x
  • Div Yield: 4%
  • P/E Forward: 4.4x
  • P/S Forward: 1.1x
  • Projected ROE 19% (Guidance)
  • Projected Div Yield: 5.5%

Following a base case where, one year from now, the company continues to trade at 5.5x P/E (LTM) and the 2025 earnings come in as expected, we can project an appreciation of 25% in one year, along with a yield of 5–6% that it should provide in the same period. I believe this scenario is the most likely.

Valuation Metrics/Scenarios:

Drivers

I believe that the two main drivers the company has are the normalization of the macro scenario and its own comments regarding its operations, which I will take as “Guidance”.

Guidance:

  • "We expect sustainable gross margins around 33% to 34%"
  • "We intend to launch more than in 2024"
  • "We expect sustainable ROE around 18% to 20% in 2025"
  • "Solid cash generation in 2025, with a target of ~R$300–500 million in free cash flow"

The other main driver would be the normalization of macro risk perception in the long term, which, as already mentioned, is projected.

Two other smaller drivers would be the expansion of the income bracket of the “Minha Casa Minha Vida” (MCMV) program, which is a housing financing program that now also includes a large portion of the Brazilian middle class, raising the income brackets to R$ 8,600 to R$ 12,000, indicating a greater tendency for sales in the middle-income segment. There is also a large volume of savings accumulated by higher-income families, allowing for cash purchases or smaller financing, and consequently making them less affected by a possible macro stagnation. In fact, 76% of the VGV launched in 2024 came from the mid- and high-end segments.

Risks

The Brazilian real estate sector is going through 2025 with a cautiously positive outlook. The Central Bank, in a statement, emphasized that the scenario requires a “significantly contractionary monetary policy for a prolonged period”. The benchmark interest rate reached 15% per year in June 2025, the highest level in almost 20 years. Everything indicates that the scenario is to gradually ease rates in late 2025 if there is confidence in the decline of prices. In other words, the cost of credit is expected to remain high in the short term.

An eventual taxation of LCI would likely increase mortgage costs and reduce the availability of cheap housing credit, negatively affecting the pace of sales in the low- and mid-income segments where credit is essential. Cyrela’s high-end segment would be less impacted given its lower reliance on financing, but overall sector dynamics could slow.

Conclusion

My Buy thesis assumes that Brazil’s monetary cycle will turn in 2025 H2. A 150–300 bps cut would likely lift sector P/Es back to 6-7 × and let Cyrela trade around US$5.70/ADR. If rates remain at 15% through 2026, we expect the stock to stay in the US$3.5-4.5 range.

However, even in a prolonged high-rate scenario, Cyrela’s earnings power—supported by its operational discipline, high-end market resilience, and strong ROE—could drive gradual appreciation. If the company continues growing net income and trades at a stable P/E of 5×, price gains would still materialize proportionally to profit growth.

The perspective of this thesis is to be paid to wait. The moment Brazil’s macro environment shows signs of improvement, appreciation should take place. Until then, it offers a 5% annual yield, 0.48 beta, and diversification into other markets.

BUY – Target Price: $5.50

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Matrix Service Company: A Backdoor LNG Play
data.description:
Today, I am putting a small cap construction and engineering concern called Matrix Service Company (NASDAQ:MTRX) in the spotlight for the first time. The stock is off nearly 20% from its highs in mid-February. Matrix's last quarterly results posted in May did miss expectations, but did see over 20% annual revenue growth. The company is doing a solid job paring losses and is projected to be profitable in 2026. Matrix also is sitting on a record order backlog and should continue to benefit from the huge expansion in LNG capacity. Is the stock now priced at a discount or will the shares head still lower? An analysis follows below.

Matrix Service Company is headquartered in Tulsa, OK. The company provides engineering, fabrication, construction, and maintenance services. The company primary customers are in the critical energy infrastructure and industrial markets in the U.S., Canada and globally. The stock trades just under $13.00 a share and sports an approximate $350 million market capitalization. The company has an unusual fiscal year that begins on July 1st, and the company is in its FY2026 year.

The company has three primary business segments. The first is Utility and Power Infrastructure and the second is Storage and Terminals Solutions. The latter is the beneficiary of the large uptick in LNG vessel and storage solution demand. The last business segment is called Process and Industrial Facilities which performs work like refinery maintenance and renewable diesel projects.

Recent Results:

Matrix Service Company posted its 3Q2025 numbers on May 7th. The company posted a non-GAAP loss of 12 cents a share, this was seven cents a share worse than the consensus. However, the net loss of $3.4 million in the quarter was a large improvement from the net loss of $14.6 million or 53 cents a share in the same period a year. Adjusted EBITDA was flat compared to a negative $10 million.

Gross margins nearly doubled to 6.4% from 3.4% in the same period a year. Management stated the improvement was largely due to higher revenue volumes in its Storage and Terminal Solutions and Utility and Power Infrastructure segments. SG&A expenses also fell to $17.7 million from $19.9 million, due mainly to a decrease in cash-settled stock-based compensation.

Revenues rose 20.6% on a year-over-year basis to just over $200 million, which miss expectations by more than $14 million. Despite the over 20% rise in sales, order backlog grew 7.7% sequentially from the previous quarter to a record $1.4 billion.

The Storage and Terminals Solutions segment drove the growth as revenue increased 77% to $96.1 million from the same period a year ago. Sales from the Utility and Power Infrastructure segment revenue rose 27% to $58.7 million. Sales did fall significantly in the Process and Industrial Facilities segments as revenues dropped $20.2 million to $45.4 million. The drop off was driven primarily by lower revenue volumes resulting from the completion of a large renewable diesel project.

Order backlog growth is also being powered by the Storage and Terminals Solution segment as can be seen above. New orders included a project for the engineering and construction of large refrigerated propane and butane tanks. Management did revise FY2025 sales guidance down $50 million down to $850 million to $900 million. This was due to due to project timing delays. Leadership also stated the company would return to profitability in the second half of this year.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Since first quarter results hit the wires, Sidoti ($16 price target) maintained its Hold rating while D.A. Davidson reissued their Buy rating. Just over two percent of the current float of the stock is currently held short. Insiders haven't been active in the stock with no insider transactions recorded since September of last year.

The company's balance sheet is pristine contains no long-term debt and listed $185 million in cash and marketable securities based on the 10-K filed for the quarter. Matrix had an additional $25 million of restricted cash.

Conclusion:

Matrix Services Company had a loss of $1.06 a share (non-GAAP) in FY2025 on just over $728 million worth of revenues. The current analyst firm consensus has losses dropping to 64 cents a share on $785 million in FY2026. Matrix is projected to turn a profit of just over 60 cents a share on sales growth of 20% in FY2027. It is important to note that only two analyst firms have projections on Matrix Services Company posted.

What I do like about Matrix Services Company is the firm has some long-term demand drivers including the LNG build out, the investment needed to upgrade the electrical grid and the increasing amount of energy needed by data centers.

Now, given my pessimistic view of the overall market, I am not adding many companies that are currently losing money. However, for Matrix I will make a slight exception. The company is moving rapidly back to profitability. In addition, it has a record order backlog which should serve it well if the economy slows markedly. Matrix Services Company also has a rock-solid balance sheet. Revenue growth also should pick up in the second half of this year. Therefore, I am going to pick up a small position in MTRX via covered call orders this week as that seems to be a prudent way to accumulate a 'watch item' holding in this interesting and evolving story.

data.title: Embracer: 'Kingdom Come: Deliverance II' Doing Well
data.description:
Embracer (OTCPK:THQQF) (OTCPK:EBCRY) had a decent quarter indeed with a strong showing from Kingdom Come: Deliverance II (KCD2) as we discussed in our previous coverage. It drove solid segment results and is performing on timelines. The pipeline is actually not terrible at the moment. Of relevant titles, we are looking at the full release for Hyperlight Breaker, Gothic Remake, Titan Quest II and Killing Floor 3. We think there is actually more ahead than behind Embracer at this moment in time and for this game development cycle, though they need some solid upcoming releases to be ready for sale within two years from this point, which they don't currently have. We believe the thinner pipeline is the reason for the company to carry a significantly lower EV/EBITDA multiple than peers like Sega Sammy (OTCPK:SGAMY) and the other U.S. majors.

In addition to that, the breakdown of Embracer continues to be in plan. This will essentially create two companies. The RemainCo seems to be the one that will be a bit more robust. It will have IPs like KCD, Metro, Dead Island, Killing Floor and basically, everything it seems that will matter in the coming cycle, including Middle Earth Enterprises. Dark Horse Comics will stay with the RemainCo (rebranded as Middle Earth Enterprises). The NewCo (Coffee Stain and Friends) will be games like Valheim, Deep Rock Galactic and Goat Simulator among some others. Both are essentially gaming businesses, but RemainCo likely has a lot more juice on both the LotR MMO that will one day come out and also the general health of their IP library. We provide a basic framework for deciding which stock to own when the time comes.

Latest Results

Asmodee is gone before the close of the books. It had its own segment in prior reporting of being tabletop games. That's gone as of the February spinoff. The effects are primarily on the overall topline and account for around two of the three months in the Q4. But we will focus on the segment reporting where there wasn't overlap.

PC/Console gaming is down, though it has nothing to do with Asmodee and everything to do with Gearbox and Saber Interactive no longer being present in results, which was part of the restructuring and capitulation of the company to assuage fears around the debt load. We covered this extensively on SA. Organic segment growth was 22% excluding these negative perimeter effects, with -2% being the headline revenue growth figure. On an adjusted basis, EBIT doubled, mainly on profitability actions with the Saber Interactive and Gearbox sales being in a period where neither of these studios had released anything while part of Embracer. The organic growth of 22% came down to a very solid release of KCD2.

In our forecast model, KCD2 had 2.7 million kr sales as of 1 month from its release. It's around 3.6 million kr now. A year out from the release, a doubling in sales from the 1-month figure is to be expected, and they are on track. It was a well-received game and a strong sequel to KCD. Dead Island 2 came out, and did reasonably well it would seem, even though it's mainly a console game it had good figures on SteamDB. Metro Released in Q3. Didn't seem to do that well compared to previous releases. Note, the yellow highlights show games that have come out, and the counts given for those games were those we forecast, not the actual performance. Another peculiarity has been Hyperlight Breaker. While a smaller game, its previous entry was a mega hit in the early days of indie devs taking over the market. It was released in early access, and therefore the figures in actuality are really quite bad compared to our forecasts. We hope that a full release eventually will bring in the bulk of the addressable market as early access games do tend to be ignored, thinking about Baldur's Gate 3.

In terms of coming releases, there would still be some juice in future results with a full release of Hyperlight Breaker, Titan Quest II, whose first entry did really well, and Killing Floor 3, which is the third entry in a really solid horror franchise. The Gothic Remake could also see traction as the franchise, while maybe mainly a cult appeal right now, was well liked back in the day. As much opportunity ahead as behind, Embracer in their games pipeline in terms of the major announced titles since last year. They should be covered for about another 12 months, but new titles are going to need to appear on the upcoming release schedule to keep this up. Completed games are outpacing the investment figures as of now, and this does need to turn in the next interim report.

None of these games are coming from studios that are going to be part of Coffee Stain & Co.

Easybrain took a big chunk out of the mobile games business. Organic growth was 30% thanks to some games releases. Adjusted EBIT got hit on user acquisition pushes. EBIT headline figures were highly boosted by the profit on the Easybrain sale. We hope EBIT comes up a bit as the longer tail of LTV is earned off the acquired players.

For the E&S segment, the figures are a little on the down in terms of EBIT due to some impairments hitting the headline figures, but underlying profitability on Freemode was affected, hence the decline in the adjusted figures as well. This segment also had the Gollum game to benefit from, as this is where the Middle Earth license is owned. To clarify, this is the IP that covers the LotR books, primarily in terms of the most bankable assets. The IP had been sliced up by the Tolkien estate, and the expanded Legendarium is owned elsewhere. Gollum was a terrible game, so there was not much benefit there. War of the Rohirrim was also a miss. There will also be a Hunt for Gollum movie at some point, directed by Andy Serkis. Maybe this will get some traction, but honestly the IP is getting a bit tired in our opinions, in part by the Amazon series, but also just with the general frustration with IP handling of a lot of titles that touched the generations growing up in the early 2000s. The LotR MMO will eventually see licensing revenues here. An Amazon studio is going to launch the LotR MMO. They actually have a decent record with MMOs, thinking about early-on hits like New World.

Bottom Line

Taking the Coffee Stain group EBITDAC, we can at least put together a chart that gives us a crude idea of which would be the part to hold at the time of a spinoff.

If you value Coffee Stain with the group multiple now, you get the market caps corresponding with this fixed multiple valuation depending on the share each company gets of the net cash balance. In reality, a higher multiple is likely deserved by the ME&F RemainCo. RemainCo is going to be called Fellowship Entertainment. They are probably expecting a lot from the LotR IP given that. We actually think that the publishing and studio portfolio business will be a big part of the future in the long term, thanks to some solid IPs there. However, this is a starting point to knowing what to expect each element will be worth at the time of the spinoff, probably towards the end of the year based on current market cap.

Otherwise, as a comprehensive group, things are getting better after the restructuring in terms of profitability. Mobile might be a little depressed, but we should be seeing decent results out of the PC/console gaming segment. The pipeline isn't fantastically full, but for the moment there is at least enough ahead to keep investors happy for about a year. But it is getting urgent for new upcoming releases with notable market power to be announced. With the general state of the industry and plenty of scrutiny on Embracer, while clearly discounted to gaming peers with less than half the multiple, there are reasons. Games take a couple of years to develop, and peers have deeper upcoming release buckets.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Minerva Needs To Delever Before The Brazilian Cattle Cycle Turns (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Minerva S.A. (OTCPK:MRVSY) 1Q25 results were the company's first full quarter of consolidated financials after the acquisition of Marfrig's beef operations in Brazil and Argentina (Uruguay pending). I think it's a good time to recap my coverage of the company after one year from my original article on the name.

The company is currently dealing with integration challenges that limit operational efficiency and weigh on capacity utilization. However, Minerva maintains healthy EBITDA margins on aggregate operations.

The main concern remains its elevated leverage, which is manageable for now, but at the edge of what's acceptable for a cyclical processor. Adding to the risk, the Brazilian beef cycle appears to be peaking and could soon turn negative. The company plans to raise R$2 billion in equity, an expected 30% dilution. For that reason, I believe the name deserves a downgrade to Hold at least until the capital increase materializes.

A challenging year

FY24 was a difficult year for Minerva and its shareholders. The primary issue was the company's decision to take on significant debt to acquire Marfrig's beef assets without immediately integrating those operations into its own platform. This caused financial stress in the form of high interest costs and an elevated risk profile.

Toward the end of the year, additional headwinds emerged. A depreciation in the BRL triggered a revaluation in cattle prices, compressing gross margins, the key lever in beef processing profitability.

Fortunately, by Q4 FY24, the company had successfully integrated its new Brazilian and Argentine facilities, representing a nameplate 30% increase in processing capacity. Still pending is regulatory approval for the Uruguayan facilities, as local antitrust authorities remain concerned that the deal would give Minerva a monopolistic position in Uruguay's beef export market.

Integration margins and utilization

Operations are not yet normalized in the acquired facilities. During the 1Q25 earnings call, management stated that Minerva's legacy plants were operating at 75% utilization, while newly acquired plants were only at 50-55%. This implies that the acquisition effectively added just 20% in usable capacity so far, with the potential to expand to 30% in the next few quarters.

In a business like beef processing, where fixed costs are high, efficient utilization is critical. Plants require trained staff, regulatory compliance, and SG&A overheads, making underutilization costly. That said, management expects to raise utilization at the new plants over the coming quarters, which would help dilute overhead and improve margins.

Despite this, consolidated EBITDA margins remain resilient at 9%, roughly in line with historical levels. Management noted that the new plants are currently operating with ~6.5% EBITDA margins, suggesting room for improvement.

To put this in context, JBS (JBS) Brazil's beef operations are currently operating at 6% margins (see 1Q25's earnings release), so Minerva's numbers are still relatively strong. Volumes are comparable, with both Minerva and JBS' Brazil Beef around $10 billion in revenues.

Debt, Minerva's Achilles' heel

Minerva's operational execution is not the issue. Its margin profile is healthier than the best peer, JBS. The problem is debt.

Following the Marfrig acquisition, Minerva now carries around R$27 billion in gross debt. Of this, R$8 billion is BRL-denominated and variable-rate, R$8 billion is USD debt swapped into BRL, and another R$12 billion is direct USD exposure. This capital structure exposes the company to both interest rate volatility and currency risk.

The financial expense is about R$3.2 billion per year, assuming Brazilian real interest rates in the 12-14% range. The company offsets this with R$12 billion in cash and marketable securities, roughly half of which is in foreign currency. This portfolio earns around R$700 million per year.

Net debt stands at just above 3x expected FY25 EBITDA (R$4.5 billion at the midpoint of guidance), a tolerable level, but the upper bound of what's considered prudent for a cyclical firm.

The cycle risk

Even with its high debt load, Minerva's interest payments (~R$2.5 billion net) are currently covered by expected EBITDA minus capex (R$800 million to R$1 billion). However, the beef cycle appears to be turning.

Brazilian beef packers like Minerva benefit when cattle are abundant and cheap. That may no longer be the case in Brazil. During Q3/Q4 last year, cattle prices rose 30% YoY because of higher BRLUSD exchange rates, but when the rates came down in Q1, prices did not decrease (see Industry Report by Minerva). Export prices kept pace, up ~30% in BRL terms and 10% in USD terms, thanks to strong demand and tight supply in the US. The USDA projects the Brazilian cattle cycle will turn in 2025, moving from expansion to contraction.

This would mean tighter supply and higher input costs for processors. If that's coupled with weaker demand from key markets like China or the US, Minerva would face margin compression from both sides. Given its leverage, this is the primary risk: that cyclical pressure erodes earnings to the point where debt becomes a problem.

Capitalization coming

To address its stretched balance sheet, Minerva plans to issue R$2 billion in equity (see Subsequent Events on the company's financial statements). That's about 50% of its current market cap (~R$4 billion) and would result in ~30% dilution for current shareholders.

The founding family (22% ownership) and the Saudi investment fund SALIC (30% ownership) have committed to subscribing to half of the new issuance. This signals confidence from the controlling shareholders and prevents control dilution.

Still, the offering is only a partial fix. Even if successful, it barely moves the needle on total debt. Minerva needs to generate sustained cash flow to meaningfully deleverage.

Valuation is not attractive

Using company guidance, Minerva could generate around R$700 million in net income this year. This assumes R$4.5 billion in EBITDA, R$1 billion in capex, R$2.5 billion in net interest, and a 30% tax rate.

With the upcoming dilution, this would translate to roughly R$0.70 per share or ~R$2.80 per ADR (about $0.50 at the current exchange rate).

That represents a 10% earnings yield on the Brazilian stock or the ADR's price. While not bad on the surface, it's less compelling in light of the company's leverage, upcoming dilution, and potential cyclical headwinds.

Moreover, given its balance sheet, the company cannot afford to return capital to shareholders. All earnings must go toward debt reduction. It's worth noting that for every R$700 million in debt that Minerva repays, it adds roughly R$65 million in post-tax earnings. That gives the stock a sort of growth yield if management can execute a sustained deleveraging plan.

Conclusion

Minerva is a high-quality beef processor with above-average EBITDA margins and good operational execution. However, it has mismanaged its capital structure. On top of that, the Brazilian cattle cycle is turning, which could pressure gross margins, the key driver of processor profitability.

The upcoming equity raise should relieve some financial pressure (very little on the aggregate), but it comes at the cost of 30% dilution. Valuation metrics look fair but not compelling, especially considering the company's need to retain earnings rather than return them to shareholders.

For now, I prefer to wait and see how the capital increase plays out and whether Minerva can raise plant utilization. Until then, I downgrade the name to a Hold rating on MRVSY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Penguin Solutions: A Quiet Contender In The AI Infrastructure Race
data.description:
Penguin Solutions is one of those lesser-covered semiconductor names that doesn’t come up often in AI investing circles, but I think it’ll become more well-known over the next few years. And that’s because even though the market is still fixated on GPU suppliers and hyperscalers, Penguin is building something quietly compelling: a high-performance computing business with real revenue growth, strong cash flow, and a clear path to scale.

What caught my attention is how quickly the company is evolving. What we are seeing is a business that’s riding demand for compute hardware and delivering end-to-end AI infrastructure with software, orchestration, and enterprise integration layered in. I believe that, given the factors I’ll outline below, it’s easy to see why this is starting to look like a durable, capital-efficient growth story.

Company Overview — Vertically Integrated, AI-Focused

Penguin Solutions (NASDAQ:PENG) is a US semiconductor company that provides high-performance computing systems tailored for AI, machine learning, and data-intensive enterprise workloads. Looking back at the company’s history, it’s best-known for its custom-built servers and clustered systems, often tailored for research labs, aerospace, and government clients.

Recently, though, the company has been evolving towards becoming a vertically integrated AI infrastructure player. We can see this with its offerings in enterprise AI infrastructure, where it bundles compute hardware, interconnect, memory, orchestration software, and support into full-stack systems under the Penguin Solutions and OriginAI brands.

Its business now operates across three main segments:

  • Advanced Computing – AI and HPC systems, including GPU-accelerated infrastructure and software (now over 50% of revenue)

  • Memory Solutions – Custom and legacy DRAM products for enterprise and industrial customers

  • LED Solutions – High-performance LED components for displays and industrial lighting

In my view, that push towards vertical integration is crucial for the next leg of PENG’s journey. The recent partnerships, like CDW for AI distribution and SK Telecom’s $200M strategic investment, are fantastic levers to help the company scale its AI offering across mainstream enterprise markets.

Q2 FY25 Highlights

Penguin posted very strong results in Q2 FY25, and most of that was led by expansive growth in its AI-driven Advanced Computing business:

  • Revenue: $366M (+28% YoY), driven by Advanced Computing growth of 42% YoY to $200M.

  • Non-GAAP Gross Margin: 30.8%

  • Non-GAAP EPS: $0.52 (up 97% YoY)

  • Operating Cash Flow: $73M

  • Cash on Hand: $647M (up from $394M QoQ)

The Case for Penguin’s Next Leg Up

AI Infrastructure Demand

First, Penguin is not selling off-the-shelf GPUs to crypto miners or small dev teams. It’s providing customized, enterprise-ready AI compute systems, coupled with software orchestration, integration support, and white-glove delivery. I believe that we can see the underlying demand here because in Q2 FY25, its Advanced Computing business surged 42% YoY. In real numbers, that came out to $200 million and representing 55% of total company revenue. That percentage increase, compared to 48% in Q1, indicates that what we have here is a fundamental shift in where the company’s growth is anchored.

Crucially, management noted on the earnings call that many of these deals are expanding from proof-of-concept deployments to full-scale enterprise rollouts. In practical terms, that means customers are no longer buying a few racks for experimentation — they’re building production-scale clusters, often customized to specific AI training and inference workloads. I can’t overstate how particularly important this is, because it suggests that PENG’s solutions are geared for recurring engagement and long-term integration.

Moreover, the market timing couldn’t be better. AI models are becoming more complex, and multi-modal workloads are expanding. This means that enterprises need and are looking for infrastructure partners who can remove any hardware integration headaches and deliver optimized, scalable platforms. Penguin is stepping directly into this gap. And the fact is that they’ve managed to do it profitably, with 30.8% non-GAAP gross margins and nearly doubling EPS YoY to $0.52. I’d say that this shows their ability to monetize this transition while maintaining financial discipline.

The way I see it, if the Advanced Computing segment maintains management’s expected 15–25% growth rate in the coming quarters, this alone would justify re-rating Penguin’s valuation multiple. Again, you have to consider how capital-efficient the current model appears.

Vertical Integration

Nvidia and other chip manufacturers are grabbing the headlines for GPU chips, but companies like Penguin are the ones building the supporting architecture around those chips.

Let’s take a look at its ICE ClusterWare™ platform and the new ICE ClusterWare AIM™ Service, for example. The former offers out-of-the-box cluster configuration, scheduling, workload management, and monitoring. The latter is a software service that uses “prediction automation designed to maximize AI performance.” When you combine the two, it turns Penguin’s offerings from commodity hardware into sticky infrastructure software platforms that are harder to rip and replace once deployed.

Why does this matter? Two reasons. First, it boosts margins: software is higher-margin than hardware. Second, it raises switching costs, which means that existing customers will come back for future deployments and expansions. At the same time, the integrated approach allows Penguin to offer a differentiated product in a crowded AI market. I can’t stress how much of an advantage this is, given that too many companies are either focused solely on silicon or solely on cloud-native software.

GTM Leverage Through Dell & Strategic Capital from SK Telecom

One of the biggest constraints for companies building physical infrastructure is scale, both in terms of supply chain and go-to-market. To that end, let’s talk about SK Telecom’s $200M investment in Penguin, which came through in December 2024. This wasn’t passive capital; the money also came with a board seat and alignment on strategic initiatives, particularly in telecom-grade AI infrastructure. It gives Penguin both the financial runway and the strategic alliance to enter new international and telecom verticals with credibility and resourcing.

We’ve also seen additional collaborations between the two companies and SK hynix “to pursue the development and delivery of comprehensive AI data center (“AIDC”) solutions.” Here’s what management had to say about it during the Q2 earnings call.

“We are in discussions with SK Telecom regarding potential collaborations related to their AI strategy, including AI data center infrastructure initiatives. In addition, an already strong relationship between SK hynix and SMART Modular is evolving as we look at ways to address markets not historically served by SK hynix and custom low-volume high-mix segments.”

Then we have Dell’s partnership with the company, which allows Penguin to expand its market reach as its AI software and managed services are now directly accessible through Dell’s global salesforce. In my opinion, this is another major sign that Penguin is maturing as a scalable enterprise infrastructure player. It’d be fair to say that the partnership is already yielding results. As CEO Mark Adams noted during the Q2 2025 earnings call, Penguin is “training Dell sales teams” and “seeing favorable early response to co-marketing opportunities with large enterprise customers.”

That is a critical advantage, especially now that enterprises are actively evaluating vendors for high-performance AI infrastructure rollouts. What’s more, Penguin has expanded its OriginAI offering to include Dell servers alongside its own ClusterWare software and services. That expansion significantly increases the company’s total addressable market and configuration flexibility for prospective clients.

Administrative Update — Redomiciling from the Cayman Islands to Delaware

Let me quickly touch on this. Penguin also announced plans to redomicile its parent company from the Cayman Islands to Delaware, and that move looks to be happening. The company filed a proxy statement regarding a Scheme of Arrangement back in May, and shareholders approved the move on June 16, so what’s left is court approval. Sure, the move is a formality, but it also has meaningful operational and investor implications. As CFO Nate Olmstead explained during the Q2 call, the redomiciliation aligns the company’s legal structure with where its actual business now lives: the U.S. hosts its headquarters, executive team, core operational assets, and the bulk of its employees.

So, the shift will simplify Penguin’s compliance and tax administration, and it also goes without saying that it could also ease engagement with US federal customers. That’s particularly important as the company may want to get deeper penetration into AI infrastructure contracts involving public-sector deployments. To be clear, Olmstead said that the company hasn’t really thought about how the move affects its government business. Still, it’s hard to ignore that U.S. incorporation often makes vendors more attractive from a procurement and partnership standpoint in federally sensitive industries.

Valuation

I especially like comparative company valuations in cases like this, where the company in question has such deep discounts to the sector median.

In this case, I’m using comparative EV/Sales ratios to value Penguin, and I’ve left the bigger players in the semiconductor industry out. Let’s focus on similarly sized businesses here. It’s clear that Penguin is grossly undervalued by any metric. Right now, it’s trading at around 0.86x EV/Sales, and that’s at a -72% difference to the sector median. Meanwhile, my valuation model indicates that even in a Bear case scenario, all things being equal, and the company’s EV/Sales ratio rises to 3x, we’ll see a target price of ~$65. That’s over triple the current price.

Conversely, I’m optimistic that, given the tailwinds I discussed above, the company can easily hit a valuation multiple of 3.5x. That gives us an upside of nearly 4x the current price. The Bull Case is even closer to 10x the current price and 6x the existing market cap. That is unrealistic at this time, but it's not out of the realm of possibility over the next few years. Again, this all hinges on the market opportunity and how Penguin executes against it, but given what we’ve seen so far, I wouldn’t bet against it.

P.S. All the figures in the valuation are denoted in USD.

Risks

While I’m bullish on Penguin’s long-term trajectory, here are the key risks that could delay the upside case:

1. Margin Compression in a Crowded AI Infrastructure Market

Penguin is competing in a fast-moving space where hardware commoditization is always a risk. Gross margins are stable, yes, (30.8% non-GAAP in Q2). Still, a broader pricing war or unexpected cost inflation, especially around GPUs, memory, or networking, could put pressure on the blended margin as it scales. If we see any lags in software adoption or its product bundling fails to generate incremental pricing power, then margins could flatten just as growth accelerates.

2. Execution Risk Around Channel Expansion

The Dell and SK Telecom partnerships unlock Penguin’s access to new markets, but they also come with dependency. So, if the go-to-market alignment stalls or we see slower demand growth with the services segment, even with these partners, that obviously affects revenue growth. We can’t discount it, even with strong product readiness, and it’s something to watch out for, especially in a hardware-centric business where revenue is still tied to large, lumpy deployments.

Investor Takeaway

Penguin Solutions isn’t trying to be the next Nvidia (frankly, I don’t know who can), and that’s exactly why I like it. The market is still focused on all the headline AI names while Penguin is quietly executing on the infrastructure that actually makes enterprise-scale AI work. We’ve seen 40%+ growth in its core segment, rising margins, strong cash flow, and credible partners like Dell, CDW, and SK Telecom behind it. As such, I strongly believe that this is a fundamentally sound business trading at a very steep discount to its trajectory.

Again, it has its risks, which I outlined above, but the bar for upside isn’t high. If the company simply continues doing what it’s already doing, I think we’ll see meaningful multiple expansion from here.

data.title: Paychex Earnings Preview: Paycor Adds Fuel, But Valuation Stays High
data.description:
Introduction

Paychex, Inc. (NASDAQ:PAYX) is about to announce its fourth-quarter report pre-market next Wednesday, carrying the weight of very high expectations. Paychex has always made its money the same simple way: handling payroll and HR headaches for small and mid-sized companies so they don’t have to. That’s kept it pretty shielded from big economic surprises over the years, and the story hasn’t really changed, until now. This time around, the Paycor acquisition is the twist that makes things interesting. Buying Paycor should push Paychex higher and broaden what they can sell, but the market already acts like this is guaranteed to go exactly as planned, which isn’t right.

Today, I will break down what to expect in the upcoming earnings, what to expect from this acquisition, and how it may impact the stock of this company.

Company Overview

To not reinvent the wheel, I will quote what the company does from its own Q3 2025 investor presentation:

Paychex is an industry-leading human capital management (HCM) company delivering a full suite of technology and advisory services in human resources, employee benefits, insurance, and payroll for small- to medium-sized businesses.

It means the company basically takes care of all the paperwork and headaches that come with having employees. They run payroll so everyone gets paid correctly and on time, handle taxes and filings, so businesses don’t mess up and get fined, help pick and manage things like health insurance and retirement plans for workers, they know and follow all of the HR rules if there’s a problem or something changes in the law. It’s all the stuff most small and mid-sized companies don’t have the time or people to do themselves, so Paychex does it for them and keeps it organized in one place.

Many analysts say that Paychex is one of the best indicators to know how healthy the U.S. job market is. Last quarter, revenue came in about five percent higher than a year ago, getting to $1.5 billion, and if you ignore the drop as the result of the ERTC program that’s finally over, the real growth was closer to six percent (the ERTC was a pandemic-era tax credit that gave businesses money back for keeping workers on payroll, so last year it still boosted Paychex’s numbers a bit, but now it’s completely gone).

Adjusted EPS grew eight percent to $1.49, which was possible thanks to the fact that they managed to keep down the costs and improve their efficiency, pushing the adjusted operating margin up to almost 47%, about 180 basis points better than last year. Clients are sticking around the same as before, and new sign-ups haven’t decreased either, even though there’s been a lot of talk lately about small businesses being money-cautious.

What to Expect on Earnings

When Paychex reports this quarter, what really matters is pretty simple. First, we’ll see if small businesses kept hiring and paying people at the same pace as before, or if things started to soften up now that everyone’s watching costs more closely. Second, I’ll look at whether the average paycheck per employee stayed about the same, went up, or dropped because that will show how stable their payroll volume really is. And third, this will be the first look at the Paycor deal numbers.

Wall Street expects Paychex to earn about $1.19 per share (normalized) and bring in around $1.41 billion in revenue for the fourth quarter. But what’s interesting is that analysts have actually lowered their EPS estimates more than they’ve raised them lately, with eleven cuts in the last three months versus only three upward updates. I think it means there is little room for imperfection, but I will talk more about it later on in this article.

On their own, Paychex said they expect this year’s revenue growth to end up at the lower end of the range they gave earlier, so around four to five and a half percent if you ignore Paycor. But with Paycor’s revenue added in the fourth quarter should come in much higher, maybe around ten to twelve percent growth. So, naturally, people want to know: is that big jump mostly just the result of adding Paycor’s sales on top, or are they actually getting more big clients and selling more services now that they have a stronger offering for mid-sized businesses? The answer is probably a little bit of both, but we won’t know for sure for some time.

Including Paycor, we expect revenue growth of 10% to 12% in the fourth quarter and the impact from the acquisition to adjusted EPS would be neutral.

Paycor Acquisition

For Paychex, spending $4.1 billion on anything is a pretty bold move, especially while interest rates are high and borrowing costs aren’t cheap. They covered it by issuing $4.2 billion in senior notes, locking in rates between 5.1% and 5.6% all the way out to 2035. That’s manageable, but for a company that has always been proud about having a rock-solid balance sheet and barely any debt, it’s a big new chunk of leverage to carry.

The reason for this acquisition, though, makes sense. Paycor brings in bigger clients, the kind that might normally pick ADP or UKG over Paychex. So adding Paycor should help Paychex chase large customers, make their TAM wider, and create more ways to cross-sell, like 401(k) plans, insurance, talent management tools, etc.

But the market thinks it will all happen much faster than it possibly can. Management is saying they’ll get to over $80 million a year in cost savings pretty quickly, which is already higher than what they first promised. They also claim the deal will boost adjusted earnings next fiscal year. That’s nice, but merging two companies is never so simple. There are always and always difficulties and investors must understand that it may be a tough time for the company.

Valuation

It’s fair to wonder if the market hasn’t already priced this in like nothing can go wrong because that’s what the market usually does on the news about mergers. Paychex right now trades at a level that doesn’t really forgive surprises, and adding more debt to the mix just makes that tighter. Just look at these numbers below. The stock is majorly overvalued compared to the sector. And it’s actually higher right now than its own 5-year average in all of the metrics, except forward PEG, which is a projection of high expectations.

They increased the dividend by over ten percent this spring, so clearly they trust the cash will keep coming, but if the merger turns out messier or slower than planned, and it absolutely can because it's a heavy task to process, the impact will be noticeable. And then there’s the simple fact that small business hiring has been steady but nothing special, with checks per client basically flat for the year. Put it together, and you get a solid, healthy company, but at this price, the stock expects more than just “solid.”

Risks

While big-picture data about small and mid-size businesses has been looking great so far, it can change at any moment. Even now, small businesses are struggling with high labor costs, and in some areas, demand isn’t exactly booming. And the conditions and the economy are not the most stable. Hiring could definitely slow, and it will show in the company’s numbers, especially now at times of a merger, when the balance sheet carries more debt.

Conclusion

Paychex is a good business. They do exactly what they promise, they don’t chase unrealistic growth, and they manage to keep their clients happy. But with this Paycor deal, they’ve given themselves a lot to digest, and at today’s price, I don’t think the stock leaves any space for difficulties. I am not saying there should be some, but it’s very likely. I like what they’re building for the long term, but in my opinion, there is a strong chance investors will see better entry points, whether it will be right after the earnings if the company doesn't satisfy the elevated expectations, or later, on some other news or market swings.

data.title: Qualcomm: Oversold Status Doesn't Make Sense, Rich Upside Potential Ahead
data.description:
QCOM Remains Inherently Discounted Despite The Recent Recovery - Near Doubling Upside Possible

We previously covered QUALCOMM (NASDAQ:QCOM) (QCOM:CA) in March 2025, discussing its inherently undervaluation with strong growth prospects in numerous end markets, despite the potential geopolitical/ macroeconomic headwinds.

Combined with the richer profit margins, the management's promising forward guidance, and the potential outperformance against the pessimistic consensus forward estimates, we had believed that the selloff had triggered a rare dollar cost averaging opportunity.

QCOM 1Y Stock Price

Since then, QCOM has breached its established 2024 floor at the $150s ranges to chart a new floor at the $124s ranges, before returning once again to the $150s by the time of writing.

Much of the recovery tailwinds are attributed to the 90-day tariff pause, with these bringing a much-needed reprieve for the semiconductor company, since China comprises 46% of its revenues in FY2024 (+9 points YoY) and the US at 25% (-4 points YoY).

Given that QCOM also relies on third-party manufacturers based in China, Taiwan, Singapore, and Germany, we can understand why the market has been undecided about its prospects, as observed in the stock's underperformance against its peers thus far.

On the other hand, we believe that the market's uncertainties have been overly done, attributed to the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war with an eventual US import tariff at 55% and China at 10% as of June 12, 2025, against the prior numbers at 145% and 125% as of April 11, 2025, respectively.

This implies that QCOM is likely to outperform the consensus' mixed estimates, as similarly reiterated by the management in the prior FQ2'25 earnings call in April 2025:

What I said is we do not see any material direct impact. There is smaller direct impact and some minor changes in demand. We are reflecting that and so it's in our numbers, but nothing that really is significant enough to change the direction of our guidance. So that's the data point I was giving. (Seeking Alpha)

These reasons are also why we believe that QCOM is likely to deliver another double beat performance in their upcoming FQ3'25 earnings call on July 30, 2025.

This is significantly aided by the H1'25 revenue outperformance at +17.1% YoY and the adj EPS outperformance at $6.26 (+20.6% YoY) along with the management's FQ3'25 revenue guidance at +9.6% YoY and the adj EPS guidance of $2.70 at the midpoint (+15.8% YoY).

At the same time, we believe that QCOM is likely to achieve its ambitious non-handset revenue growth target from $14.1B in FY2024 to $22B by FY2029, expanding at a CAGR of +9.3%, based on the H1'25 non-handset revenue growth rate at +22.1 YoY and FY2024 at +6.1% YoY.

Combined with the likely to be accretive Alphawave Semi acquisition (AI connectivity), Edge Impulse (AI capabilities for IoT), and FocusAI (AI-enabled video capabilities) on its existing compute/ AI/ data center connectivity and SaaS capabilities, we believe that the company is likely to continue generating robust double digits growth profile while shedding the prior smartphone mantle.

This is on top of the +59% YoY revenue growth observed in the automotive segment with an FY2029 automotive revenue target of $8B, aided by the growing backlog for smart glasses with an FY2029 XR revenue target of $2B, with it underscoring QCOM's excellent revenue diversification thus far.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are also why we believe that the consensus forward estimates above appear to be overly pessimistic, with QCOM merely expected to report a top/ bottom-line growth projection at a CAGR of +5.4%/ +6% through FY2027.

This is notably decelerated compared to the 5Y historical growth at +15%/ +23.6% and 10Y growth at +3.9%/ +6.8%, respectively, with the market notably negating their historical performance and the management's promising guidance.

While there may be near-term headwinds on smartphone sales, with QCOM's share of Apple's (AAPL) product businesses in fall 2025 likely to be lower at 70%, we believe that QCOM is likely to be well insulated in the near-term.

Our optimism is well-supported by its H1'25 handset revenues growth by +12.6% along with the management's FQ3'25 handset revenue growth guidance of +10% YoY, aided by the growing partnership across major Android OEMS and the higher government subsidies in China, with the latter "expected to see 3% year-over-year growth."

QCOM Valuations

These reasons are also why we believe that QCOM remains extremely cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 13.13x, compared to the 1Y mean of 14.82x, the 5Y mean of 16.14x, and the 10Y mean of 15.41x, despite the recent recovery from the P/E bottom of 10.66x in April 2025.

This is especially since the company is increasingly becoming a force to be reckoned with in the CPU/ AI/ networking segment, with the stock still inherently discounted compared to the sector P/E median of 22.54x and its peers, including:

  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 32.87x
  • Nvidia (NVDA) at 33.62x,
  • Broadcom (AVGO) at 37.68x,
  • Marvell (MRVL) at 25.03x, and
  • Arm (ARM) at 81.23x,
  • aside from Intel (INTC) at a 5Y pre-pandemic P/E non-GAAP mean of 12.77x.

Assuming that QCOM is able to sustain the adj EPS expansion profile at a normalized growth rate of +10% (based on a similar rate as the non-handset and handset 5Y revenue growth guidance), we are looking at an estimated FY2027 adj EPS of $13.60 and an estimated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.31x.

When compared to the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.76x and its semiconductor peers, including AMD at 1.26x, NVDA at 1.18x, AVGO at 1.65x, MRVL at 0.58x, and ARM at 2.97x, it is apparent that QCOM remains reasonably valued at current levels, with it offering interested investors with an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is QCOM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

QCOM 5Y Stock Price

For now, QCOM's 5Y stock price chart is not very encouraging indeed, despite the robust growth prospects for the non-handset segment as the management seeks to diversify its long-term prospects through numerous acquisitions.

On the other hand, it is undeniable that the stock has been oversold as the bulls also defend the $124s ranges and the FWD P/E valuations of 10.66x as the new floors during the worst of the April 2025 meltdown.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $11.28 (+24.2% YoY) and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 13.13x, it is apparent that the QCOM stock is still trading near to our base-case fair value estimates of $148.10.

There remains a decent upside potential of +16.1% to our base-case long-term price target of $178.50 as well, based on our estimated FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $13.60.

QCOM's Oversold Status

At the same time, we believe that QCOM remains inherently discounted at current levels, as similarly observed on the stock's historical technical indicators above, with the recent bottoming likely to support its upward momentum ahead.

Assuming a further upgrade in its valuations to the sector median of 22x (still overly cheap compared to the peer group mean of 32x), we believe that the stock now offers an extremely rich near doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $299.20.

These numbers are not overly aggressive indeed, aided by the moderation in QCOM's short interest volumes by -30.3% from 2024 heights along with the established floor at the $124s and the subsequent recovery to $153s ranges by the time of writing.

While its stock price reversal may be prolonged, patient investors may enjoy the richer forward dividend yields of 2.32% compared to the sector median of 1.62%, with the management's robust capital use also observed in -1.3% of its float retired over the LTM and -7.9% since FY2019.

As a result of the robust dual pronged return prospects across capital appreciation and dividend incomes, we are reiterating our Strong Buy rating for the QCOM stock.
